:mod:`The IndexedDimension class` 
=================================

.. module:: slicer.dimensions

.. versionadded:: 0.1
.. moduleauthor:: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

.. class:: IndexedDimension(Dimension)

Summary
-------

    The IndexDimension class implements a special dimension which assumes that
    the values in the dimension are sorted and spaced equally, typically a latitude
    and a longitude on a resular grid. The index of a values can thus be calculated
    instead of searched, this is much more efficient.

    The IndexDimension class implements two more methods which can be overridden
    by its possible descendants. This assumes that the values contained in the
    dimension are not the actual values used by the user, but a transposition. A 
    typical example would be a date where the array for the dimension contains offsets
    from a reference date. Those methods are called everytime a conversion is needed:

    ..method:: codeToValue(code):

        :param code: code if the value stored in the dimension (e.g. an offset for the date). 

        In that case, codeToValue should return a date.

    ..method:: valueToCode(value):

        :param value: value is the value manipulated by the user (e.g. a date).

        In that case, valueToCode should return an offset from a reference date.

    Another method used internally should be documented as it could be useful:

    ..method:: indice(value,where='left'):

        :param value: the value we want the index of
        :param where: preferences for the match ('left','right','nearest')
        :type where: string

        This method returns the index of a value in the dimension variable. If the value is
        not in the variable, three options can be chosen:

            - return the index of the value on its left 
            - return the index of the value on its right
            - return the index of the nearest value

        The first two options are useful when selecting an interval while the third
        one is useful for discrete values.
